annotations.label: has pathogen
annotations.definition: A host interaction where the smaller of the two members of a symbiosis causes a disease in the larger member

annotations.label: has evidence
annotations.definition: inverse of is evidence for
annotations.comment: x has evidence y iff , x is  an information content entity, material entity or process, and y supports either the existence of x, or the truth value of x.

annotations.label: results in movement of
annotations.definition: Holds between p and c when p is locomotion process and the outcome of this process is the change of location of c

annotations.label: causally influences
annotations.definition: The entity or characteristic A is causally upstream of the entity or characteristic B, A having an effect on B. An entity corresponds to any biological type of entity as long as a mass is measurable. A characteristic corresponds to a particular specificity of an entity (e.g., phenotype, shape, size).

annotations.label: biomechanically related to
annotations.definition: A relation that holds between elements of a musculoskeletal system or its analogs.

annotations.label: has muscle antagonist
annotations.definition: m1 has_muscle_antagonist m2 iff m1 has_muscle_insertion s, m2 has_muscle_insection s, m1 acts in opposition to m2, and m2 is responsible for returning the structure to its initial position.

annotations.label: has branching part
annotations.definition: inverse of branching part of

annotations.label: conduit for
annotations.definition: x is a conduit for y iff y overlaps through the lumen_of of x, and y has parts on either side of the lumen of x.

annotations.label: lumen of
annotations.definition: x lumen_of y iff x is the space or substance that is part of y and does not cross any of the inner membranes or boundaries of y that is maximal with respect to the volume of the convex hull.

annotations.label: luminal space of
annotations.definition: s is luminal space of x iff s is lumen_of x and s is an immaterial entity

annotations.label: has modifier
annotations.definition: A relation that holds between an attribute or a qualifier and another attribute.

annotations.label: participates in a biotic-biotic interaction with
skeleton of
annotations.definition: inverse of has skeleton

annotations.label: directly regulates
annotations.definition: p directly regulates q iff p is immediately causally upstream of q and p regulates q.

annotations.label: existence starts at end of
annotations.definition: holds between x and y if and only if the time point at which x starts is equivalent to the time point at which y ends. Formally: iff α(x) = ω(y).

annotations.label: has part structure that is capable of
annotations.definition: s 'has part structure that is capable of' p if and only if there exists some part x such that s 'has part' x and x 'capable of' p

annotations.label: results in breakdown of
annotations.definition: p results in breakdown of c if and only if the execution of p leads to c no longer being present at the end of p

annotations.label: results in catabolism of
annotations.definition: p results in catabolism of c if and only if p is a catabolic process, and the execution of p results in c being broken into smaller parts with energy being released.

annotations.label: results in organization of
annotations.definition: p results in organization of c iff p results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of c

annotations.label: existence ends at start of
annotations.definition: holds between x and y if and only if the time point at which x ends is equivalent to the time point at which y starts. Formally: iff ω(x) = α(y).

annotations.label: causal relation between material entity and a process
annotations.definition: A relationship that holds between a material entity and a process in which causality is involved, with either the material entity or some part of the material entity exerting some influence over the process, or the process influencing some aspect of the material entity.

annotations.label: capable of regulating
annotations.definition: Holds between c and p if and only if c is capable of some activity a, and a regulates p.

annotations.label: capable of negatively regulating
annotations.definition: Holds between c and p if and only if c is capable of some activity a, and a negatively regulates p.

annotations.label: capable of positively regulating
annotations.definition: Holds between c and p if and only if c is capable of some activity a, and a positively regulates p.

annotations.label: capable of inhibiting or preventing pathological process
annotations.definition: Holds between a material entity c and a pathological process p if and only if c is capable of some activity a, where a inhibits p.

annotations.label: capable of upregulating or causing pathological process
annotations.definition: Holds between a material entity c and a pathological process p if and only if c is capable of some activity a, where a negatively regulates p.

annotations.label: is substance that treats
annotations.definition: c is a substance that treats d if c is a material entity (such as a small molecule or compound) and d is a pathological process, phenotype or disease, and c is capable of some activity that negative regulates or decreases the magnitude of d.

annotations.label: is marker for
annotations.definition: c is marker for d iff the presence or occurrence of d is correlated with the presence of occurrence of c, and the observation of c is used to infer the presence or occurrence of d. Note that this does not imply that c and d are in a direct causal relationship, as it may be the case that there is a third entity e that stands in a direct causal relationship with c and d.

annotations.label: process has causal agent
annotations.definition: Inverse of 'causal agent in process'

annotations.label: correlated with
annotations.definition: A relationship that holds between two entities, where the entities exhibit a statistical dependence relationship. The entities may be statistical variables, or they may be other kinds of entities such as diseases, chemical entities or processes.

annotations.label: is evidence with support from
annotations.definition: A relationship between a piece of evidence and an entity that plays a role in supporting that evidence.
annotations.comment: In the Gene Ontology association model, this corresponds to the With/From field

annotations.label: has model
annotations.definition: Inverse of is-model-of

annotations.label: related via evidence or inference to
directly positively regulates
annotations.definition: p directly positively regulates q iff p is immediately causally upstream of q, and p positively regulates q.

annotations.label: directly negatively regulates
annotations.definition: p directly negatively regulates q iff p is immediately causally upstream of q, and p negatively regulates q.

annotations.label: ectoparasite of
annotations.definition: A sub-relation of parasite-of in which the parasite lives on or in the integumental system of the host

annotations.label: has ectoparasite
annotations.definition: inverse of ectoparasite of

annotations.label: mesoparasite of
annotations.definition: A sub-relation of parasite-of in which the parasite is partially an endoparasite and partially an ectoparasite

annotations.label: has mesoparasite
annotations.definition: inverse of mesoparasite of

annotations.label: intercellular endoparasite of
annotations.definition: A sub-relation of endoparasite-of in which the parasite inhabits the spaces between host cells.

annotations.label: has intercellular endoparasite
annotations.definition: inverse of intercellular endoparasite of

annotations.label: intracellular endoparasite of
annotations.definition: A sub-relation of endoparasite-of in which the parasite inhabits host cells.

annotations.label: has intracellular endoparasite
annotations.definition: inverse of intracellular endoparasite of

annotations.label: co-roosts with
annotations.definition: Two or more individuals sharing the same roost site (cave, mine, tree or tree hollow, animal burrow, leaf tent, rock crack, space in man-made structure, etc.). Individuals that are sharing a communal roost may be said to be co-roosting. The roost may be either a day roost where the individuals rest during daytime hours, or a night roost where individuals roost to feed, groom, or rest in between flights and/or foraging bouts.  Communal roosting as thus defined is an umbrella term within which different specialized types -- which are not mutually exclusive -- may be recognized based on taxonomy and the temporal and spatial relationships of the individuals that are co-roosting.

annotations.label: reservoir host of
annotations.definition: A relation between a host organism and a hosted organism in which the hosted organism naturally occurs in an indefinitely maintained reservoir provided by the host.
annotations.comment: A reservoir is a population, species or community (assemblage of different species in a given geographic area) in which a microorganism naturally occurs and is indefinitely maintained. Some zoonotic pathogens, particularly bacterial pathogens, may also have environmental reservoirs. Microorganisms with multiple reservoir species may be indefinitely maintained across the community of species even if they are not always present in each individual reservoir species. In the reservoir species or community, the microorganism may cause either asymptomatic infection or disease and this may vary among individuals. A pathogen may also be more genetically diverse in its reservoir host than in other taxa, in part because the pathogen is endemic to reservoir hosts.

annotations.label: has reservoir host
annotations.definition: inverse of reservoir host of

annotations.label: ectomycorrhizal host of
annotations.definition: x is an ectomycorrhizal host of y if and only if x is a plant (Plantae) whose roots form an intercellular interface (aka Hartig net) with fungus (Fungi) y. This interface consists of highly branched hyphae forming a latticework between epidermal and cortical root cells.
annotations.comment: An ectomycorrhiza is a form of symbiotic relationship that occurs between a fungal symbiont, or mycobiont, and the roots of various plant species. The mycobiont is often from the phyla Basidiomycota and Ascomycota, and more rarely from the Zygomycota. Ectomycorrhizae form on the roots of around 2% of plant species, usually woody plants, including species from the birch, dipterocarp, myrtle, beech, willow, pine and rose families. Research on ectomycorrhizae is increasingly important in areas such as ecosystem management and restoration, forestry, and agriculture. Unlike other mycorrhizal relationships, such as arbuscular mycorrhiza and ericoid mycorrhiza, ectomycorrhizal fungi do not penetrate their host's cell wall. Instead they form an entirely intercellular interfaces known as the Hartig net, consisting of highly branched hyphae forming a latticework between epidermal and cortical root cells.

annotations.label: has ectomycorrhizal host
annotations.definition: inverse of ectomycorrhizal host of

annotations.label: arbuscular mycorrhizal host of
annotations.definition: x is an arbuscular mycorrhizal host of y if and only if x is a vascular plant (Plantae) whose cortical cells of the roots are penetrated by fungus (Fungi) y and form arbuscules.
annotations.comment: An arbuscular mycorrhiza (AM) (plural mycorrhizae) is a type of mycorrhiza in which the symbiont fungus (Arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi, or AMF) penetrates the cortical cells of the roots of a vascular plant forming arbuscules. Arbuscular mycorrhiza is a type of endomycorrhiza along with ericoid mycorrhiza and orchid mycorrhiza (not to be confused with ectomycorrhiza). They are characterized by the formation of unique tree-like structures, the arbuscules. In addition, globular storage structures called vesicles are often encountered. Arbuscular mycorrhizae are formed by fungi in the subphylum Glomeromycotina and some fungi from the Mucoromycotina. These subphyla, along with the Mortierellomycotina, form the phylum Mucoromycota, a sister clade of the more well-known and diverse dikaryan fungi. AM fungi help plants to capture nutrients such as phosphorus, sulfur, nitrogen and micronutrients from the soil. It is believed that the development of the arbuscular mycorrhizal symbiosis played a crucial role in the initial colonisation of land by plants and in the evolution of the vascular plants. It has been said that it is quicker to list the plants that do not form endomycorrhizae than those that do. This symbiosis is a highly evolved mutualistic relationship found between fungi and plants, the most prevalent plant symbiosis known, and AMF is found in 80% of vascular plant families in existence today.

annotations.label: has arbuscular mycorrhizal host
annotations.definition: inverse of arbuscular mycorrhizal host of

annotations.label: produces
annotations.definition: a produces b if some process that occurs_in a has_output b, where a and b are material entities. Examples: hybridoma cell line produces monoclonal antibody reagent; chondroblast produces avascular GAG-rich matrix.
annotations.comment: Note that this definition doesn't quite distinguish the output of a transformation process from a production process, which is related to the identity/granularity issue.

annotations.label: produced by
annotations.definition: a produced_by b iff some process that occurs_in b has_output a.

annotations.label: represses expression of
annotations.definition: Holds between entity A (a transcription factor) and a nucleic acid B if and only if A down-regulates the expression of B. The nucleic acid can be a gene or an mRNA.

annotations.label: increases expression of
annotations.definition: Holds between entity A (a transcription factor) and nucleic acid B if and only if A up-regulates the expression of B. The nucleic acid can be a gene or mRNA.

annotations.label: has role in modeling
annotations.definition: A relation between a biological, experimental, or computational artifact and an entity it is used to study, in virtue of its replicating or approximating features of the studied entity.
annotations.comment: The primary use case for this relation was to link a biological model system such as a cell line or model organism to a disease it is used to investigate, in virtue of the model system exhibiting features similar to that of the disease of interest. But the relation is defined more broadly to support other use cases, such as linking genes in which alterations are made to create model systems to the condition the system is used to interrogate, or computational models to real-world phenomena they are defined to simulate.

annotations.label: causes or contributes to condition
annotations.definition: A relationship between an entity (e.g. a genotype, genetic variation, chemical, or environmental exposure) and a condition (a phenotype or disease), where the entity has some causal or contributing role that influences the condition.
annotations.comment: Genetic variations can span any level of granularity from a full genome or genotype to an individual gene  or sequence alteration.  These variations can be represented at the physical level (DNA/RNA macromolecules or their parts, as in the ChEBI ontology and Molecular Sequence Ontology) or at the abstract level (generically dependent continuant sequence features that are carried by these macromolecules, as in the Sequence Ontology and Genotype Ontology).  The causal relations in this hierarchy can be used in linking either physical or abstract genetic variations to phenotypes or diseases they cause or contribute to.\n\nEnvironmental exposures include those imposed by natural environments, experimentally applied conditions, or clinical interventions.

annotations.label: causes condition
annotations.definition: A relationship between an entity (e.g. a genotype, genetic variation, chemical, or environmental exposure) and a condition (a phenotype or disease), where the entity has some causal role for the condition.

annotations.label: contributes to condition
annotations.definition: A relationship between an entity (e.g. a genotype, genetic variation, chemical, or environmental exposure) and a condition (a phenotype or disease), where the entity has some contributing role that influences the condition.

annotations.label: contributes to severity of condition
annotations.definition: A relationship between an entity (e.g. a genotype, genetic variation, chemical, or environmental exposure) and a condition (a phenotype or disease), where the entity influences the severity with which a condition manifests in an individual.

annotations.label: contributes to frequency of condition
annotations.definition: A relationship between an entity (e.g. a genotype, genetic variation, chemical, or environmental exposure) and a condition (a phenotype or disease), where the entity influences the frequency of the condition in a population.

annotations.label: ameliorates condition
annotations.definition: A relationship between an entity (e.g. a genotype, genetic variation, chemical, or environmental exposure) and a condition (a phenotype or disease), where the presence of the entity reduces or eliminates some or all aspects of the condition.
annotations.comment: Genetic variations can span any level of granularity from a full genome or genotype to an individual gene  or sequence alteration.  These variations can be represented at the physical level (DNA/RNA macromolecules or their parts, as in the ChEBI ontology and Molecular Sequence Ontology) or at the abstract level (generically dependent continuant sequence features that are carried by these macromolecules, as in the Sequence Ontology and Genotype Ontology).  The causal relations in this hierarchy can be used in linking either physical or abstract genetic variations to phenotypes or diseases they cause or contribute to. \n\nEnvironmental exposures include those imposed by natural environments, experimentally applied conditions, or clinical interventions.

annotations.label: correlated with condition
annotations.definition: A relationship between an entity and a condition (phenotype or disease) with which it exhibits a statistical dependence relationship.

annotations.label: exacerbates condition
annotations.definition: A relationship between an entity (e.g. a chemical, environmental exposure, or some form of genetic variation) and a condition (a phenotype or disease), where the presence of the entity worsens some or all aspects of the condition.

annotations.label: condition ameliorated by
annotations.definition: A relationship between a condition (a phenotype or disease) and an entity (e.g. a chemical, environmental exposure, or some form of genetic variation) where some or all aspects of the condition are reduced or eliminated by the presence of the entity.

annotations.label: condition exacerbated by
annotations.definition: A relationship between a condition (a phenotype or disease) and an entity (e.g. a chemical, environmental exposure, or some form of genetic variation) where some or all aspects of the condition are worsened by the presence of the entity.

annotations.label: has material basis in gain of function germline mutation in
has material basis in loss of function germline mutation in
has material basis in germline mutation in
has material basis in somatic mutation in
has partial material basis in germline mutation in
has primary input or output
annotations.definition: p 'has primary input ot output' c iff either (a) p 'has primary input' c or (b) p 'has primary output' c.

annotations.label: has primary output
annotations.definition: p has primary output c if (a) p has output c and (b) the goal of process is to modify, produce, or transform c.

annotations.label: has primary input
annotations.definition: p has primary input c if (a) p has input c and (b) the goal of process is to modify, consume, or transform c.

annotations.label: is causal gain of function germline mutation of in
annotations.definition: Relates a gene to condition, such that a mutation in this gene in a germ cell provides a new function of the corresponding product and that is sufficient to produce the condition and that can be passed on to offspring[modified from orphanet].

annotations.label: is causal loss of function germline mutation of in
annotations.definition: Relates a gene to condition, such that a mutation in this gene in a germ cell impairs the function of the corresponding product and that is sufficient to produce the condition and that can be passed on to offspring[modified from orphanet].

annotations.label: is causal germline mutation in
annotations.definition: Relates a gene to condition, such that a mutation in this gene is sufficient to produce the condition and that can be passed on to offspring[modified from orphanet].

annotations.label: is causal somatic mutation in
annotations.definition: Relates a gene to condition, such that a mutation in this gene is sufficient to produce the condition but that cannot be passed on to offspring[modified from orphanet].

annotations.label: is causal susceptibility factor for
annotations.definition: Relates a gene to condition, such that a mutation in this gene predisposes to the development of a condition  and that is necessary but not sufficient to develop the condition[modified from orphanet].

annotations.label: is causal germline mutation partially giving rise to
annotations.definition: Relates a gene to condition, such that a mutation in this gene partially contributes to the presentation of this condition[modified from orphanet].

annotations.label: disease has basis in dysfunction of
annotations.definition: A relation that holds between the disease and a material entity where the physical basis of the disease is a disorder of that material entity that affects its function.

annotations.label: disease has basis in disruption of
annotations.definition: A relation that holds between the disease and a process where the physical basis of the disease disrupts execution of a key biological process.

annotations.label: disease has basis in feature
annotations.definition: A relation that holds between the disease and a feature (a phenotype or other disease) where the physical basis of the disease is the feature.

annotations.label: causal relationship with disease as subject
disease causes disruption of
annotations.definition: A relationship between a disease and a process where the disease process disrupts the execution of the process.

annotations.label: disease has location
annotations.definition: A relationship between a disease and an anatomical entity where the disease has one or more features that are located in that entity.

annotations.label: disease has inflammation site
annotations.definition: A relationship between a disease and an anatomical entity where the disease is triggered by an inflammatory response to stimuli occurring in the anatomical entity

annotations.label: realized in response to stimulus
annotations.definition: A relationship between a realizable entity R (e.g. function or disposition) and a material entity M where R is realized in response to a process that has an input stimulus of M.

annotations.label: disease has feature
annotations.definition: A relationship between a disease and some feature of that disease, where the feature is either a phenotype or an isolated disease.

annotations.label: disease arises from alteration in structure
annotations.definition: A relationship between a disease and an anatomical structure where the material basis of the disease is some pathological change in the structure. Anatomical structure includes cellular and sub-cellular entities, such as chromosome and organelles.

annotations.label: enables subfunction
annotations.definition: Holds between an entity and an process P where the entity enables some larger compound process, and that larger process has-part P.

annotations.label: acts upstream of or within, positive effect
acts upstream of or within, negative effect
acts upstream of, positive effect
annotations.definition: c 'acts upstream of, positive effect' p if c is enables f, and f is causally upstream of p, and the direction of f is positive

annotations.label: acts upstream of, negative effect
annotations.definition: c 'acts upstream of, negative effect' p if c is enables f, and f is causally upstream of p, and the direction of f is negative

annotations.label: causally upstream of or within, negative effect
causally upstream of or within, positive effect
has driver
annotations.definition: A relation between two entities, in which one of the entities is any natural or human-influenced factor that directly or indirectly causes a change in the other entity.

annotations.label: has disease driver
annotations.definition: A relation between an entity and a disease of a host, in which the entity is not part of the host itself, and the condition results in pathological processes.

annotations.label: epiphyte of
annotations.definition: An interaction relationship wherein a plant or algae is living on the outside surface of another plant.

annotations.label: has epiphyte
annotations.definition: inverse of epiphyte of

annotations.label: kleptoparasite of
annotations.definition: A sub-relation of parasite of in which a parasite steals resources from another organism, usually food or nest material

annotations.label: kleptoparasitized by
annotations.definition: inverse of kleptoparasite of

annotations.label: creates habitat for
annotations.definition: An interaction relationship wherein one organism creates a structure or environment that is lived in by another organism.

annotations.label: ecologically co-occurs with
annotations.definition: An interaction relationship describing organisms that often occur together at the same time and space or in the same environment.

annotations.label: lays eggs on
annotations.definition: An interaction relationship in which organism a lays eggs on the outside surface of organism b. Organism b is neither helped nor harmed in the process of egg laying or incubation.

annotations.label: has eggs laid on by
annotations.definition: inverse of lays eggs on

annotations.label: has roost
annotations.definition: x 'has roost' y if and only if: x is an organism, y is a habitat, and y can support rest behaviors x.
annotations.comment: A population of xs will possess adaptations (either evolved naturally or via artifical selection) which permit it to rest in y.

annotations.label: symbiotrophically interacts with
annotations.definition: A trophic interaction in which one organism acquires nutrients through a symbiotic relationship with another organism

annotations.label: trophic parasite of
annotations.definition: A symbiotrophic interaction in which one organism acquires nutrients through a parasitic relationship with another organism

annotations.label: has substance added
annotations.definition: "has substance added" is a relation existing between a (physical) entity and a substance in which the entity has had the substance added to it at some point in time.

annotations.label: has substance removed
annotations.definition: "has substance removed" is a relation existing between two physical entities in which the first entity has had the second entity (a substance) removed from it at some point in time.

annotations.label: immersed in
annotations.definition: "immersed in" is a relation between a (physical) entity and a fluid substance in which the entity is wholely or substantially surrounded by the substance.

annotations.label: has consumer
annotations.definition: 'has consumer' is a relation between a material entity and an organism in which the former can normally be digested or otherwise absorbed by the latter without immediate or persistent ill effect.

annotations.label: has primary substance added
annotations.definition: 'has primary substance added' indicates that an entity has had the given substance added to it in a proportion greater than any other added substance.

annotations.label: assay measures characteristic
annotations.definition: A relation between an assay and a characteristic, in which the assay generates a data item which is a measure of a characteristic.

annotations.label: characteristic measured by assay
annotations.definition: Inverse of 'assay measures characteristic'

annotations.label: realized in response to
annotations.definition: r 'realized in response to' s iff, r is a realizable (e.g. a plant trait such as responsivity to drought), s is an environmental stimulus (a process), and s directly causes the realization of r.

annotations.label: generically depends on
annotations.definition: A generically dependent continuant *b* generically depends on an independent continuant *c* at time *t* means: there inheres in *c* a specifically deendent continuant which concretizes *b* at *t*.

annotations.label: is carrier of
annotations.definition: *b* is carrier of *c* at time *t* if and only if *c* *g-depends on* *b* at *t*

annotations.label: regulates activity of
annotations.definition: The entity A has an activity that regulates an activity of the entity B. For example, A and B are gene products where the catalytic activity of A regulates the kinase activity of B.

annotations.label: regulates quantity of
annotations.definition: The entity A has an activity that regulates the quantity or abundance or concentration of the entity B.

annotations.label: indirectly regulates activity of
annotations.definition: The entity A is not immediately upstream of the entity B but A has an activity that regulates an activity performed by B.

annotations.label: decreases by repression quantity of
annotations.definition:
The entity A has an activity that down-regulates by repression the quantity of B. The down-regulation is due to A having an effect on an intermediate entity (typically a DNA or mRNA element) which can produce B.

For example, protein A (transcription factor) indirectly decreases by repression the quantity of protein B (gene product) if and only if A negatively regulates the process of transcription or translation of a nucleic acid element that produces B.

annotations.label: increases by expression quantity of
annotations.definition:
The entity A has an activity that up-regulates by expression the quantity of B. The up-regulation is due to A having an effect on an intermediate entity (typically a DNA or mRNA element) which can produce B.

For example, protein A (transcription factor) indirectly increases by expression the quantity of protein B (gene product) if and only if A positively regulates the process of transcription or translation of a nucleic acid element that produces B.

annotations.label: directly positively regulates quantity of
annotations.definition: The entity A has an activity that directly positively regulates the quantity of B.

annotations.label: directly negatively regulates quantity of
annotations.definition: The entity A has an activity that directly negatively regulates the quantity of B.

annotations.label: indirectly positively regulates activity of
annotations.definition: The entity A is not immediately upstream of the entity B and has an activity that up-regulates an activity performed by B.

annotations.label: destabilizes quantity of
annotations.definition: An entity A directly interacts with B and A has an activity that decreases the amount of an entity B by degradating it.

annotations.label: stabilizes quantity of
annotations.definition: An entity A physically interacts with B and A has an activity that increases the amount of an entity B by stabilizing it.

annotations.label: indirectly negatively regulates activity of
annotations.definition: The entity A is not immediately upstream of the entity B and has an activity that down-regulates an activity performed by B.

annotations.label: directly regulates quantity of
annotations.definition: The entity A, immediately upstream of B, has an activity that directly regulates the quantity of B.

annotations.label: indirectly regulates quantity of
annotations.definition: The entity A is not immediately upstream of the entity B, but A has an activity that regulates the quantity or abundance or concentration of B.

annotations.label: indirectly negatively regulates quantity of
annotations.definition: The entity A does not physically interact with the entity B, and A has an activity that down-regulates the quantity or abundance or concentration of B.

annotations.label: indirectly positively regulates quantity of
annotations.definition: The entity A does not physically interact with the entity B, and A has an activity that up-regulates the quantity or abundance or concentration of B.

annotations.label: has small molecule regulator
annotations.definition: a relation between a process and a continuant, in which the process is regulated by the small molecule continuant

annotations.label: has small molecule activator
annotations.definition: a relation between a process and a continuant, in which the process is activated by the small molecule continuant

annotations.label: has small molecule inhibitor
annotations.definition: a relation between a process and a continuant, in which the process is inhibited by the small molecule continuant

annotations.label: acts on population of
annotations.definition: p acts on population of c iff c' is a collection, has members of type c, and p has participant c

annotations.label: is small molecule regulator of
annotations.definition: a relation between a continuant and a process, in which the continuant is a small molecule that regulates the process

annotations.label: is small molecule activator of
annotations.definition: a relation between a continuant and a process, in which the continuant is a small molecule that activates the process

annotations.label: is small molecule inhibitor of
annotations.definition: a relation between a continuant and a process, in which the continuant is a small molecule that inhibits the process

annotations.label: results in adhesion of
annotations.definition: The relationship that links anatomical entities with a process that results in the adhesion of two or more entities via the non-covalent interaction of molecules expressed in, located in, and/or adjacent to, those entities.

annotations.label: constitutively upstream of
annotations.definition: p is constitutively upstream of q iff p is causally upstream of q, p is required for execution of q or a part of q, and the execution of p is approximately constant.

annotations.label: removes input for
annotations.definition: p removes input for q iff p is causally upstream of q, there exists some c such that p has_input c and q has_input c, p reduces the levels of c, and c is rate limiting for execution of q.

annotations.label: indirectly causally upstream of
annotations.definition: p is indirectly causally upstream of q iff p is causally upstream of q and there exists some process r such that p is causally upstream of r and r is causally upstream of q.

annotations.label: indirectly regulates
annotations.definition: p indirectly regulates q iff p is indirectly causally upstream of q and p regulates q.

annotations.label: positively regulates in another organism
annotations.definition: x 'positively regulates in other organism' y if and only if: (x is the realization of a function to increase the frequency, rate or extent of y) AND (the agents of x are produced by organism o1 and the agents of y are produced by organism o2).

annotations.label: negatively regulates in another organism
annotations.definition: x 'negatively regulates in other organism' y if and only if: (x is the realization of a function to reduce the frequency, rate or extent of y) AND (the agents of x are produced by organism o1 and the agents of y are produced by organism o2).

annotations.label: has synaptic IO in region
annotations.definition: A relationship between a neuron and a region, where the neuron has a functionally relevant number of input and/or output synapses in that region.

synonyms.text: has synaptic input or output in

annotations.label: receives synaptic input in region
annotations.definition: A relationship between a neuron and a region, where the neuron has a functionally relevant number of input synapses in that region.

annotations.label: sends synaptic output to region
annotations.definition: A relationship between a neuron and a region, where the neuron has a functionally relevant number of output synapses in that region.

annotations.label: has synaptic IO throughout
annotations.definition: A relationship between a neuron and a region, where the neuron has a functionally relevant number of input and/or output synapses distributed throughout that region (rather than confined to a subregion).

annotations.label: receives synaptic input throughout
annotations.definition: A relationship between a neuron and a region, where the neuron has a functionally relevant number of input synapses distributed throughout that region (rather than confined to a subregion).

annotations.label: sends synaptic output throughout
annotations.definition: A relationship between a neuron and a region, where the neuron has a functionally relevant number output synapses distributed throughout that region (rather than confined to a subregion).

annotations.label: has sensory dendrite in
annotations.definition: Relation between a sensory neuron and some structure in which it receives sensory input via a sensory dendrite.

annotations.label: receives synaptic input from neuron
annotations.definition: A relationship between an anatomical structure (including cells) and a neuron that has a functionally relevant number of chemical synapses to it.

annotations.label: sends synaptic output to cell
annotations.definition: A relationship between a neuron and a cell that it has a functionally relevant number of chemical synapses to.
annotations.comment: Not restricting range to 'cell' - object may be a muscle containing a cell targeted by the neuron.

annotations.label: synapses restricted to region
annotations.definition: A relationship between a neuron and a region, where all of the functionally relevant input and output synapses are in that region.
annotations.comment: This relation may still be used if a very small number of synapses fall slightly outside the boundaries of the region, but any class using this relation should not have another restriction using this relation to another non-overlapping region.

annotations.label: disease has infectious agent
annotations.definition: A relationship between a disease and an infectious agent where the material basis of the disease is an infection with some infectious agent.

annotations.label: has exemplar data
annotations.definition: A relation between a material entity and some data in which the data is taken as exemplifying the material entity.

annotations.label: subcluster of
annotations.definition: A relation between a group and another group it is part of but does not fully constitute.

annotations.label: has characterizing marker set
annotations.definition: A relation that applies between a cell type and a set of markers that can be used to uniquely identify that cell type.

annotations.label: different in magnitude relative to
annotations.definition: q1 different_in_magnitude_relative_to q2 if and only if magnitude(q1) NOT =~ magnitude(q2). Here, magnitude(q) is a function that maps a quality to a unit-invariant scale.

annotations.label: increased in magnitude relative to
annotations.definition: q1 increased_in_magnitude_relative_to q2 if and only if magnitude(q1) > magnitude(q2). Here, magnitude(q) is a function that maps a quality to a unit-invariant scale.
annotations.comment: This relation is used to determine the 'directionality' of relative qualities such as 'increased strength', relative to the parent type, 'strength'.

annotations.label: decreased in magnitude relative to
annotations.definition: q1 decreased_in_magnitude_relative_to q2 if and only if magnitude(q1) < magnitude(q2). Here, magnitude(q) is a function that maps a quality to a unit-invariant scale.
annotations.comment: This relation is used to determine the 'directionality' of relative qualities such as 'decreased strength', relative to the parent type, 'strength'.

annotations.label: similar in magnitude relative to
annotations.definition: q1 similar_in_magnitude_relative_to q2 if and only if magnitude(q1) =~ magnitude(q2). Here, magnitude(q) is a function that maps a quality to a unit-invariant scale.

annotations.label: has cross section
annotations.definition: s3 has_cross_section s3 if and only if : there exists some 2d plane that intersects the bearer of s3, and the impression of s3 upon that plane has shape quality s2.
annotations.comment: Example: a spherical object has the quality of being spherical, and the spherical quality has_cross_section round.

annotations.label: reciprocal of
annotations.definition: q1 reciprocal_of q2 if and only if : q1 and q2 are relational qualities and a phenotype e q1 e2 mutually implies a phenotype e2 q2 e.
annotations.comment: There are frequently two ways to state the same thing: we can say 'spermatocyte lacks asters' or 'asters absent from spermatocyte'. In this case the quality is 'lacking all parts of type' - it is a (relational) quality of the spermatocyte, and it is with respect to instances of 'aster'. One of the popular requirements of PATO is that it continue to support 'absent', so we need to relate statements which use this quality to the 'lacking all parts of type' quality.

annotations.label: has high plasma membrane amount
annotations.definition: A relation between a cell and molecule or complex such that every instance of the cell has a high number of instances of that molecule expressed on the cell surface.

annotations.label: has low plasma membrane amount
annotations.definition: A relation between a cell and molecule or complex such that every instance of the cell has a low number of instances of that molecule expressed on the cell surface.

annotations.label: has disease
annotations.definition: A relationship that holds between an organism and a disease. Here a disease is construed broadly as a disposition to undergo pathological processes that exists in an organism because of one or more disorders in that organism.

annotations.label: has exposure medium
annotations.definition: X has exposure medium Y if X is an exposure event (process), Y is a material entity, and the stimulus for X is transmitted or carried in Y.

annotations.label: device utilizes material
annotations.definition: X device utilizes material Y means X and Y are material entities, and X is capable of some process P that has input Y.

annotations.label: information specifies
annotations.definition: A relation between information and an entity such that the information serves as a rule or guide if the entity is an occurrent, or the information serves as a model if the entity is a continuant.

annotations.label: positively correlated with
annotations.definition: A relation between entities in which one increases or decreases as the other does the same.

annotations.label: negatively correlated with
annotations.definition: A relation between entities in which one increases as the other decreases.

annotations.label: contains measured amount
annotations.definition: A relation between a container and measurement datum that specifies the actual amount of material in the container.

annotations.label: has maximum capacity
annotations.definition: A relation that relates a container to a measurement datum that specifies the maximum capacity of the container. Capacity can refer to either weight or volume.

annotations.label: symptom of
annotations.definition: inverse of 'has symptom'

annotations.label: owns
annotations.definition: A primitive relation that holds between entities x and y in which y is at x's full disposal.

annotations.label: is owned by
annotations.definition: Inverse of the owns relation.

annotations.label: specified by information
annotations.definition: Inverse of information specifies.

annotations.label: has marker
annotations.definition: Inverse relation of 'is marker for'.

annotations.label: collected during
annotations.definition: A relation between a material entity and an occurrent in which the material entity is collected during the existence of the occurrent.

annotations.label: is myristoyltransferase activity
annotations.definition: Helper relation for OWL definition of RO:0018002 myristoylates

annotations.label: myristoylates
annotations.definition: A molecularly-interacts-with relationship between two entities, where the subject catalyzes a myristoylation activity that takes the object as input

annotations.label: myristoylated by
annotations.definition: inverse of myristoylates

annotations.label: is agonist of
annotations.definition: a relation between a ligand (material entity) and a receptor (material entity) that implies the binding of the ligand to the receptor activates some activity of the receptor

annotations.label: is inverse agonist of
annotations.definition: a relation between a ligand (material entity) and a receptor (material entity) that implies the binding of the ligand to the receptor inhibits some activity of the receptor to below basal level

annotations.label: is antagonist of
annotations.definition: a relation between a ligand (material entity) and a receptor (material entity) that implies the binding of the ligand to the receptor reduces some activity of the receptor to basal level

annotations.label: is direct conjugate base of
annotations.definition: A is a direct conjugate base of B if and only if A is chemical entity that is a Brønsted–Lowry Base (i.e., can receive a proton) and by receiving a particular proton transforms it into B.

annotations.label: is direct conjugate acid of
annotations.definition: A is a direct conjugate acid of B if and only if A is chemical entity that is a Brønsted–Lowry Acid (i.e., can give up a proton) and by removing a particular proton transforms it into B.

annotations.label: is deprotonated form of
annotations.definition:
A is a deprotonated form of B if and only if A is chemical entity that is a Brønsted–Lowry Base (i.e., can receive a proton) and by adding some nonzero number of protons transforms it into B.

This is a transitive relationship and follows this design pattern: https://oborel.github.io/obo-relations/direct-and-indirect-relations.

annotations.label: is protonated form of
annotations.definition:
A is a protonated form of B if and only if A is chemical entity that is a Brønsted–Lowry Acid (i.e., can give up a proton) and by removing some nonzero number of protons transforms it into B.

This is a transitive relationship and follows this design pattern: https://oborel.github.io/obo-relations/direct-and-indirect-relations.

annotations.label: is matched small molecular pair with
annotations.definition:
A and B are a matched small molecular pair (MMP) if their chemical structures define by a single, relatively small, well-defined structural modification.

While this is normally called "matched molecular pair" in the cheminformatics literaturel, it is labeled as "matched small molecular pair" so as to reduce confusion with peptides and other macromolecules, which are also referenced as "molecules" in some contexts.

This relationship is symmetric, meaning if A is a MMP with B iff B is a MMP with A.

This relationship is not transitive, meaning that A is a MMP with B and B is a MMP with C, then A is not necessarily an MMP with C.

annotations.label: is tautomer of
annotations.definition:
Two chemicals are tautomers if they can be readily interconverted.

This commonly refers to prototropy in which a hydrogen's position is changed, such as between ketones and enols. This is also often observed in heterocyclic rings, e.g., ones containing nitrogens and/or have aryl functional groups containing heteroatoms.

annotations.label: is substitutent group from
annotations.definition: Group A is a substituent group from Chemical B if A represents the functional part of A and includes information about where it is connected. A is not itself a chemical with a fully formed chemical graph, but is rather a partial graph with one or more connection points that can be used to attach to another chemical graph, typically as a functionalization.

annotations.label: has functional parent
annotations.definition:
Chemical A has functional parent Chemical B if there is chemical transformation through which chemical B can be produced from chemical A. 

For example, the relationship between a salt and a freebased compound is a "has functional parent" relationship.

annotations.label: is enantiomer of
annotations.definition:
Chemicals A and B are enantiomers if they share the same molecular graph except the change of the configuration of substituents around exactly one chiral center.

A chemical with no chiral centers can not have an enantiomer. A chemical with multiple chiral centers can have multiple enantiomers, but its enantiomers are not themselves enantiomers (they are diastereomers).

annotations.label: has parent hydride
annotations.definition: Chemical A has parent hydride Chemical B if there exists a molecular graphical transformation where functional groups on A are replaced with hydrogens in order to yield B.

annotations.label: regulates characteristic
annotations.definition: A relationship that holds between a process and a characteristic in which process (P) regulates characteristic (C) iff: P results in the existence of C OR affects the intensity or magnitude of C.

annotations.label: positively regulates characteristic
annotations.definition: A relationship that holds between a process and a characteristic in which process (P) positively regulates characteristic (C) iff: P results in an increase in the intensity or magnitude of C.

annotations.label: negatively regulates characteristic
annotations.definition: A relationship that holds between a process and a characteristic in which process (P) negatively regulates characteristic (C) iff: P results in a decrease in the intensity or magnitude of C.

annotations.label: has substitutable entity
annotations.definition: Relates one individual to another which is capable of standing in for the first individual in its other relations to other individuals.

annotations.label: confers susceptibility to condition
annotations.definition: Relates a gene to condition, such that a variation in this gene predisposes to the development of a condition.

annotations.label: vessel supplies blood to
annotations.definition: x vessel supplies blood to y if and only if x is a vessel that supplies blood directly or indirectly to an anatomical structure y.

annotations.label: vessel drains blood from
annotations.definition: x vessel drains blood from y if and only if x is a vessel that drains blood directly or indirectly from an anatomical structure y.

annotations.label: directly supplies and drains
annotations.definition: x directly supplies and drains y if and only if x is a capillary, y is an anatomical entity, and x directly supplies and drains y.

annotations.label: is neuron
annotations.definition: This property only applies to neurons

annotations.label: is anatomical entity
annotations.definition: This property only applies to anatomical entities.

annotations.label: has numerator
annotations.definition: A relationship between a ratio or proportion and its dividend.

annotations.label: has denominator
annotations.definition: A relationship between a ratio or proportion and its divisor.

annotations.label: has allelopath
annotations.definition: inverse of allelopath of

annotations.label: provides clinical information for
annotations.definition: A relationship between a biomarker 'b' and a disease or condition of clinical interest 'd', where the presence or occurrence of 'b' provides clinical information about 'd'.
annotations.comment: Usage note: This does not imply that 'b' and 'd' are in a direct causal relationship, as it may be the case that there is a third entity 'a' that stands in a direct causal relationship with 'b' and 'd'.\n\nBasis: This relation is an umbrella for a set of relations that are based on the 'BEST' classification of and definition for 'biomarker', as given by the FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group: A defined characteristic that is measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or biological responses to an exposure or intervention, including therapeutic interventions. The BEST classification includes diagnostic, prognostic, susceptibility/risk, monitoring, predictive, safety, and pharmacodynamic/response biomarkers. See PMID:29405771 for details. Note that any given biomarker can be of a different type in different contexts.

annotations.label: diagnostic for
annotations.definition: A relationship between a biomarker 'b' and a disease or condition of clinical interest 'd', where the presence or occurrence of 'b' is used to infer the presence or occurrence of 'd' or determine its subtype.
annotations.comment: Basis: This relation is based on the 'BEST' classification of and definition for 'biomarker', as given by the FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group: A defined characteristic that is measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or biological responses to an exposure or intervention, including therapeutic interventions. The BEST classification includes diagnostic, prognostic, susceptibility/risk, monitoring, predictive, safety, and pharmacodynamic/response biomarkers. See PMID:29405771 for details. Note that any given biomarker can be of a different type in different contexts.

annotations.label: monitors status of
annotations.definition: A relationship between a serially-measured biomarker 'b' and a disease or condition of clinical interest 'd', where 'b' is used to infer the status of 'd'.
annotations.comment: Usage note: The status of 'd' refers to disease progression/remission or treatment response.\n\nBasis: This relation is based on the 'BEST' classification of and definition for 'biomarker', as given by the FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group: A defined characteristic that is measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or biological responses to an exposure or intervention, including therapeutic interventions. The BEST classification includes diagnostic, prognostic, susceptibility/risk, monitoring, predictive, safety, and pharmacodynamic/response biomarkers. See PMID:29405771 for details. Note that any given biomarker can be of a different type in different contexts.

annotations.label: indicates response to
annotations.definition: A relationship between a biomarker 'b' and an exposure agent 'e', where 'b' is used to show that a biological response to 'e' has occurred.
annotations.comment: Clarification: An 'exposure agent' can be either a medical product or some substance (e.g., pesticide) , physical factor (e.g., radiation), or organism (e.g., virus) that originates from the environment.\n\nBasis: This relation is based on the 'BEST' classification of and definition for 'biomarker', as given by the FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group: A defined characteristic that is measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or biological responses to an exposure or intervention, including therapeutic interventions. The BEST classification includes diagnostic, prognostic, susceptibility/risk, monitoring, predictive, safety, and pharmacodynamic/response biomarkers. See PMID:29405771 for details. Note that any given biomarker can be of a different type in different contexts.

annotations.label: predicts effect of
annotations.definition: A relationship between a biomarker 'b' and an exposure agent 'e', where 'b' is used to identify individuals who are likely to experience a favorable or unfavorable effect from exposure to 'e'.

annotations.label: prognostic for
annotations.definition: A relationship between a biomarker 'b' and a disease or condition of clinical interest 'd', where the presence or occurrence of 'b' is used to infer the likelihood of a clinical event, disease recurrence or progression in patients who have 'd'.

annotations.label: assesses toxicity of
annotations.definition: A relationship between a biomarker 'b' and an exposure agent 'e', where 'b' is used to indicate the likelihood, presence, or extent of toxicity as an adverse effect of exposure to 'e'.
annotations.comment: Clarification: An 'exposure agent' can be either a medical product or some substance (e.g., pesticide) , physical factor (e.g., radiation), or organism (e.g., virus) that originates from the environment.\n\nBasis: This relation is based on the 'BEST' classification of and definition for 'biomarker', as given by the FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group: A defined characteristic that is measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or biological responses to an exposure or intervention, including therapeutic interventions. The BEST classification includes diagnostic, prognostic, susceptibility/risk, monitoring, predictive, safety, and pharmacodynamic/response biomarkers. See PMID:29405771 for details. Note that any given biomarker can be of a different type in different contexts.

annotations.label: indicates risk of developing
annotations.definition: A relationship between a biomarker 'b' and a disease or condition of clinical interest 'd', where the presence or occurrence of 'b' is used to infer the potential for developing 'd' in an individual that currently does not have 'd'.

annotations.label: indicates clinical effect of
annotations.definition: A relationship between a biomarker 'b' and an exposure agent 'e', where the effect of exposure to 'e' is correlated with the presence or occurrence of 'b', and the observation of 'b' is used to infer exposure to 'e'.
annotations.comment:
Clarification: An 'exposure agent' can be either a medical product or some substance (e.g., pesticide) , physical factor (e.g., radiation), or organism (e.g., virus) that originates from the environment.

Usage note: This does not imply that 'b' and exposure to 'e' are in a direct causal relationship, as it may be the case that there is a third entity 'a' that stands in a direct causal relationship with 'b' and exposure to 'e'.

Basis: This relation is an umbrella for a set of relations that are based on the 'BEST' classification of and definition for 'biomarker', as given by the FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group: A defined characteristic that is measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or biological responses to an exposure or intervention, including therapeutic interventions. The BEST classification includes diagnostic, prognostic, susceptibility/risk, monitoring, predictive, safety, and pharmacodynamic/response biomarkers. See PMID:29405771 for details. Note that any given biomarker can be of a different type in different contexts.

annotations.label: monitors effect of
annotations.definition: A relationship between a serially-measured biomarker 'b' and an exposure agent 'e', where 'b' is used to provide evidence of a biological effect of 'e'.
annotations.comment: Clarification: An 'exposure agent' can be either a medical product or some substance (e.g., pesticide) , physical factor (e.g., radiation), or organism (e.g., virus) that originates from the environment.\n		\nBasis: This relation is based on the 'BEST' classification of and definition for 'biomarker', as given by the FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group: A defined characteristic that is measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or biological responses to an exposure or intervention, including therapeutic interventions. The BEST classification includes diagnostic, prognostic, susceptibility/risk, monitoring, predictive, safety, and pharmacodynamic/response biomarkers. See PMID:29405771 for details. Note that any given biomarker can be of a different type in different contexts.

annotations.label: monitors exposure to
annotations.definition: A relationship between a serially-measured biomarker 'b' and an exposure agent 'e', where 'b' is used to provide evidence of exposure to 'e'.

annotations.label: has constituent monomer
annotations.definition: A relation that holds between a polymer and its constituent monomers. P has_constituent_monomer 'm' if and only if P has_component multiple instances of 'm' and each instance of 'm' is covalently attached to another instance of 'm'.
annotations.comment: Usage note: The constituent monomers need not be covalently attached in an end-to-end manner; that is, polymers with internal connections (e.g., polyubiquitin) can use this relation, as can circular polymers (e.g., plasmids) and multi-branched polymers (e.g., glycans). This relation, as non-transitive, allows the number of instances of 'm' to be indicated.

annotations.label: covalently bound to
annotations.definition: A relation that holds between two distinct chemical entities that are held in close proximity to each other specifically via the force of covalent interactions.
annotations.comment: Such entities will colocalize at all times of the interaction.

annotations.label: non-covalently bound to
annotations.definition: A relation that holds between two distinct chemical entities that are held in close proximity to each other specifically via the force of non-covalent interactions.
annotations.comment: Such entities will colocalize at all times of the interaction.

annotations.label: disease relationship
annotations.definition: This relation groups  relations between diseases and any other kind of entity.

annotations.label: results in changes to anatomical or cellular structure
annotations.definition: p has anatomical participant c iff p has participant c, and c is an anatomical entity

annotations.label: in similarity relationship with
annotations.definition: Relation between biological objects that resemble or are related to each other sufficiently to warrant a comparison.

annotations.label: in homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Similarity that results from common evolutionary origin.
annotations.comment: This broad definition encompasses all the working definitions proposed so far in the literature.

annotations.label: in homoplasy relationship with
annotations.definition: Similarity that results from independent evolution.

annotations.label: in homocracy relationship with
annotations.definition: Similarity that is characterized by the organization of anatomical structures through the expression of homologous or identical patterning genes.
annotations.comment: Homology and homocracy are not mutually exclusive. The homology relationships of patterning genes may be unresolved and thus may include orthologues and paralogues.

annotations.label: in convergence relationship with
annotations.definition: Homoplasy that involves different underlying mechanisms or structures.
annotations.comment: Convergence usually implies a notion of adaptation.

annotations.label: in parallelism relationship with
annotations.definition: Homoplasy that involves homologous underlying mechanisms or structures.
annotations.comment: Can be applied for features present in closely related organisms but not present continuously in all the members of the lineage.

annotations.label: in structural homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Homology that is defined by similarity with regard to selected structural parameters.

annotations.label: in historical homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Homology that is defined by common descent.

annotations.label: in biological homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Homology that is defined by sharing of a set of developmental constraints, caused by locally acting self-regulatory mechanisms of differentiation, between individualized parts of the phenotype.
annotations.comment: Applicable only to morphology. A certain degree of ambiguity is accepted between biological homology and parallelism.

annotations.label: in reversal relationship with
annotations.definition: Homoplasy that involves phenotypes similar to those seen in ancestors within the lineage.

annotations.label: in syntenic homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Structural homology that is detected by similarity in content and organization between chromosomes.

annotations.label: in paralogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves genes that diverged after a duplication event.

annotations.label: in syntenic paralogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Paralogy that involves sets of syntenic blocks.

annotations.label: in syntenic orthology relationship with
annotations.definition: Syntenic homology that involves chromosomes of different species.

annotations.label: in partial homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Structural homology that involves complex structures from which only a fraction of the elements that can be isolated are separately homologous.

annotations.label: in protein structural homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Structural homology that is detected at the level of the 3D protein structure, but maybe not at the level of the amino acid sequence.

annotations.label: in non functional homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Structural homology that involves a pseudogenic feature and its functional ancestor.

annotations.label: in orthology relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves genes that diverged after a speciation event.
annotations.comment: The term is sometimes also used for anatomical structures.

annotations.label: in xenology relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that is characterized by an interspecies (horizontal) transfer since the common ancestor.
annotations.comment: The term is sometimes also used for anatomical structures (e.g. in case of a symbiosis).

annotations.label: in 1 to 1 homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves two members sharing no other homologs in the lineages considered.

annotations.label: in 1 to 1 orthology relationship with
annotations.definition: Orthology that involves two genes that did not experience any duplication after the speciation event that created them.

annotations.label: in ohnology relationship with
annotations.definition: Paralogy that results from a whole genome duplication event.

annotations.label: in in-paralogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Paralogy that results from a lineage-specific duplication subsequent to a given speciation event.

annotations.label: in out-paralogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Paralogy that results from a duplication preceding a given speciation event.

annotations.label: in pro-orthology relationship with
annotations.definition: 1:many orthology that involves a gene in species A and one of its ortholog in species B, when duplications more recent than the species split have occurred in species B but not in species A.

annotations.label: in semi-orthology relationship with
annotations.definition: 1:many orthology that involves a gene in species A and its ortholog in species B, when duplications more recent than the species split have occurred in species A but not in species B.
annotations.comment: The converse of pro-orthologous.

annotations.label: in serial homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Iterative homology that involves structures arranged along the main body axis.

annotations.label: in heterochronous homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Biological homology that is characterized by changes, over evolutionary time, in the rate or timing of developmental events of homologous structures.

annotations.label: in paedomorphorsis relationship with
annotations.definition: Heterochronous homology that is produced by a retention in adults of a species of traits previously seen only in juveniles.

annotations.label: in peramorphosis relationship with
annotations.definition: Heterochronous homology that is produced by a maturation of individuals of a species past adulthood, which take on hitherto unseen traits.

annotations.label: in progenesis relationship with
annotations.definition: Paedomorphosis that is produced by precocious sexual maturation of an organism still in a morphologically juvenile stage.

annotations.label: in neoteny relationship with
annotations.definition: Paedomorphosis that is produced by a retardation of somatic development.

annotations.label: in mimicry relationship with
annotations.definition: Convergence that results from co-evolution usually involving an evolutionary arms race.

annotations.label: in 1 to many orthology relationship with
annotations.definition: Orthology that involves two genes when duplications more recent than the species split have occurred in one species but not the other.

annotations.label: in many to many homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves two members of a larger set of homologs.

annotations.label: in 1 to many homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves a structure that has no other homologs in the species in which it is defined, and several homologous structures in another species.

annotations.label: in apomorphy relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that is based on recent shared ancestry, characterizing a monophyletic group.

annotations.label: in plesiomorphy relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that is based on distant shared ancestry.
annotations.comment: This term is usually contrasted to apomorphy.

annotations.label: in deep homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Homocracy that involves morphologically and phylogenetically disparate structures that are the result of parallel evolution.
annotations.comment: Used for structures in distantly related taxa.

annotations.label: in hemiplasy relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that is characterized by topological discordance between a gene tree and a species tree attributable to the phylogenetic sorting of genetic polymorphisms across successive nodes in a species tree.

annotations.label: in gametology relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves not recombining and subsequently differentiated sex chromosomes.

annotations.label: in chromosomal homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves the chromosomes able to pair (synapse) during meiosis.

annotations.label: in many to many orthology relationship with
annotations.definition: Orthology that involves two genes that experienced duplications more recent than the species split that created them.

annotations.label: in within-species paralogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Paralogy that involves genes from the same species.

annotations.label: in between-species paralogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Paralogy that involves genes from different species.
annotations.comment: The genes have diverged before a speciation event.

annotations.label: in postdisplacement relationship with
annotations.definition: Paedomorphosis that is produced by delayed growth of immature structures into the adult form.

annotations.label: in hypermorphosis relationship with
annotations.definition: Peramorphosis that is produced by a delay in the offset of development.

annotations.label: in synology relationship with
annotations.definition: Xenology that results, not from the transfer of a gene between two species, but from a hybridization of two species.

annotations.label: in isoorthology relationship with
annotations.definition: Orthology that involves functional equivalent genes with retention of the ancestral function.

annotations.label: in tandem paralogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Paralogy that is characterized by duplication of adjacent sequences on a chromosome segment.

annotations.label: in latent homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Parallelism that involves morphologically very similar structures, occurring only within some members of a taxon and absent in the common ancestor (which possessed the developmental basis to develop this character).
annotations.comment: Used for structures in closely related taxa.

annotations.label: in syngeny relationship with
annotations.definition: Homocracy that involves recognizably corresponding characters that occurs in two or more taxa, or as a repeated unit within an individual.
annotations.comment: Cannot be used when orthologous patterning gene are organizing obviously non-homologous structures in different organisms due for example to pleiotropic functions of these genes.

annotations.label: in apparent orthology relationship with
annotations.definition: Between-species paralogy that involves single copy paralogs resulting from reciprocal gene loss.
annotations.comment: The genes are actually paralogs but appear to be orthologous due to differential, lineage-specific gene loss.

annotations.label: in pseudoparalogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Xenology that involves genes that ended up in a given genome as a result of a combination of vertical inheritance and horizontal gene transfer.
annotations.comment: These genes may come out as paralogs in a single-genome analysis.

annotations.label: in equivalogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves functional equivalent genes with retention of the ancestral function.
annotations.comment: This may include examples of orthology, paralogy and xenology.

annotations.label: in interology relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves orthologous pairs of interacting molecules in different organisms.

annotations.label: in functional equivalence relationship with
annotations.definition: Similarity that is characterized by interchangeability in function.

annotations.label: in iterative homology relationship with
annotations.definition: Biological homology that involves parts of the same organism.

annotations.label: in paraxenology relationship with
annotations.definition: Xenology that is characterized by multiple horizontal transfer events, resulting in the presence of two or more copies of the foreign gene in the host genome.

annotations.label: in plerology relationship with
annotations.definition: Paralogy that is characterized by extra similarity between paralogous sequences resulting from concerted evolution.
annotations.comment: This phenomenon is usually due to gene conversion process.

annotations.label: in homotopy relationship with
annotations.definition: Structural homology that involves structures with the same or similar relative positions.
annotations.comment: Theissen (2005) mentions that some authors may consider homotopy to be distinct from homology, but this is not the standard use.

annotations.label: in homeosis relationship with
annotations.definition: Biological homology that involves an ectopic structure and the normally positioned structure.

annotations.label: in homoeology relationship with
annotations.definition: Synology that results from allopolyploidy.
annotations.comment: On a long term, it is hard to distinguish allopolyploidy from whole genome duplication.

annotations.label: in paramorphism relationship with
annotations.definition: Iterative homology that involves two structures, one of which originated as a duplicate of the other and co-opted the expression of patterning genes of the ancestral structure.

annotations.label: in regulogy relationship with
annotations.definition: Historical homology that involves orthologous pairs of transcription factors and downstream regulated genes in different organisms.
